note_data.title: Thermal properties of matter
keyPoints: **Linear expansion** occurs when a solid is heated and increases in length due to increased molecular vibrations
**Thermal expansion** can be useful (in bimetallic strips for thermostats) or cause problems (railway tracks buckling)
**Specific heat capacity** is the energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C
During **melting** or **boiling**, temperature remains constant even though heat energy is still being supplied
**Latent heat of fusion** is the energy needed to change 1 kg of solid to liquid at constant temperature
**Latent heat of vaporization** is the energy needed to change 1 kg of liquid to gas at constant temperature
**Evaporation** occurs at any temperature from the surface only, while **boiling** occurs at a fixed temperature throughout the liquid
**Cooling by evaporation** occurs because the fastest-moving molecules escape, leaving slower molecules with lower average kinetic energy

definitions.definition: The amount of thermal energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C
The amount of thermal energy needed to change 1 kg of a solid into a liquid at constant temperature
The amount of thermal energy needed to change 1 kg of a liquid into a gas at constant temperature
The increase in size of a substance when its temperature increases

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show formula, substitute values, calculate answer with correct unit
Give a sequence of steps or detailed account of what happens

modelAnswer.question: A 2.0 kg block of copper is heated and its temperature rises from 20°C to 80°C. The specific heat capacity of copper is 380 J/(kg°C). Calculate the thermal energy supplied to the copper.
modelAnswer.answer:
Thermal energy = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change
Thermal energy = 2.0 × 380 × (80 - 20)
Thermal energy = 2.0 × 380 × 60 = 45,600 J

commonMistakes: Never say 'heat capacity' — always say 'specific heat capacity' for the defined quantity
Students often forget that during melting/boiling, temperature stays constant despite energy input
Never confuse evaporation (surface only, any temperature) with boiling (throughout liquid, fixed temperature)
Students often forget to calculate temperature change (final - initial) in specific heat capacity calculations
Never say latent heat 'increases temperature' — latent heat changes state at constant temperature
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially specific heat capacity calculations
Always show the formula first, then substitute values, then calculate for full marks
Temperature-time graphs for heating are very common - remember flat sections show state changes
Learn the difference between evaporation and boiling - this distinction is frequently tested
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Temperature-time graph showing heating curve with flat sections during melting and boiling, demonstrating that temperature remains constant during state changes despite continuous energy input
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain why ice is less dense than water and describe the arrangement of molecules during melting
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — a multiple choice question about which state of matter has the strongest intermolecular forces
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) — students had to draw heating curves for water and explain what happens at each stage including latent heat
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — candidates were asked to calculate specific heat capacity using experimental data and explain sources of error

workedExample.question: A 2.0 kg block of aluminium at 80°C is placed in 5.0 kg of water at 20°C. Calculate the final temperature when thermal equilibrium is reached. (Specific heat capacity of aluminium = 900 J/(kg°C), specific heat capacity of water = 4200 J/(kg°C)) [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Set up the principle that heat lost by aluminium = heat gained by water
Step 2: Write the equation: mₐcₐ(Tᵢ - Tf) = mwcw(Tf - Tᵢ) where Tf is final temperature
workedExample.answer: The final temperature when thermal equilibrium is reached is 24.7°C
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct principle, 1 mark for correct equation setup, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused specific heat capacity with latent heat' — always check the question asks for energy during temperature change (specific heat) versus energy during state change (latent heat)
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to include units in thermal calculations' — always include J/kg for latent heat and J/(kg°C) for specific heat capacity in your final answers

Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was using incorrect temperature differences in calorimetry' — remember to use (final - initial) temperature for the substance gaining heat and (initial - final) for the substance losing heat
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a heating curve graph showing temperature vs time, with horizontal plateaus at melting and boiling points where latent heat is being supplied. The graph should show: solid phase with gradual temperature rise, horizontal melting plateau, liquid phase with steeper temperature rise, horizontal boiling plateau, and gas phase. Label axes clearly with 'Temperature (°C)' and 'Time (s)', mark melting point and boiling point temperatures, and add annotations explaining that during plateaus, energy goes into breaking bonds rather than increasing temperature.
note_data.quickRecap: Three states of matter differ in particle arrangement and energy. Specific heat capacity (c) measures energy needed to raise 1kg by 1°C using Q=mcΔT. Latent heat measures energy for state changes at constant temperature using Q=mL. In thermal equilibrium problems, heat lost equals heat gained.
